target.name: (-)-lucidumone
target.motivation: Structural complexity and potential biological activity (COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitory effects) make it an attractive synthetic target.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: oxidative dearomatization/intramolecular Diels–Alder cycloaddition and acid-promoted dynamic kinetic resolution cyclization (racemic)
enantioselective inverse-electron-demand Diels–Alder reaction (IEDDA) and a one pot retro-[4+2]/[4+2] cycloaddition sequence ((+)-lucidumone)
one-pot Claisen rearrangement/intramolecular aldolization for the construction of the tetracyclic framework (racemic and enantioselective via chiral transfer)

key_disconnections.reasoning: Connects the bicyclo[2.2.2]octane moiety and the benzene ring.

key_disconnections.named_strategy: Cu-catalyzed enantioselective silicon-tethered intramolecular Diels–Alder cycloaddition
key_disconnections.reasoning: Assembles the bicyclo[2.2.2]octane moiety with high regio- and stereoselectivity, and provides handles for secondary alcohol and methyl ketone.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy focuses on constructing the complex polycyclic core through a sequence of powerful reactions: an enantioselective silicon-tethered intramolecular Diels–Alder reaction for the bicyclo[2.2.2]octane, a Suzuki coupling to connect the two major fragments, and a tandem O-deprotection/Prins cyclization/Cycloetherification/oxidation sequence to form the tetrahydrofuran and fused indanone rings. Late-stage modifications include Fleming-Tamao oxidation, Wacker-type oxidation, and o-demethylation.

thought.background: The known diene 14 and easily accessible silyl acrylate imide 13 are available.
thought.gap: Need to form the silyl ether 12, which is the precursor for the Diels-Alder reaction.
thought.decision: To form silyl ether 12, chose Sieburth's protocol using triflic acid and 2,6-lutidine, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: Silyl ether 12 was obtained in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: Following the protocol developed by Sieburth37, exposure of the readily available silyl acrylate imide 13 to triflic acid, followed by the addition of the known primary alcohol 14 afforded silyl ether 12 in 91% yield.

thought.background: Silyl ether 12 is prepared. Intramolecular Diels-Alder cycloaddition is a key step for bicyclo[2.2.2]octane formation.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselective cycloaddition, chose Cu-catalyzed reaction with chiral C2-symmetric Box ligands, expecting high yield and enantioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Cycloadduct 11 was isolated in 96% yield with 92% ee. The ee value could be further improved to 99% after recrystallization.

evidence.text_span: Screening the reaction conditions by varying the reaction temperature, the ligands, and the copper sources led to the identification of the optimized reaction condition using Cu(OTf)2 (10 mol%) and ligand (S,S)-L1 (11 mol%) at 50 °C (see Supplementary Table 1). Under these conditions, cycloadduct 11 was isolated in 96% yield with 92% ee. Performing the reaction at 3.0 mmol in the presence of Cu(OTf)2 (0.3 mmol), (S,S)-L1 (0.33 mmol) afforded 11 without obvious erosion of yield and enantioselectivity (95% yield, 92% ee). The ee value of 11 could be further improved to 99% after recrystallization.

thought.background: Cycloadduct 11 is available. Need to hydrolyze the imide to carboxylic acid 15.
thought.decision: To hydrolyze 11, chose LiOH and H2O2 in THF/H2O, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Carboxylic acid 15 was obtained in 94% yield.

evidence.text_span: Hydrolysis of 11 (LiOH, H2O2, THF/H2O v/v = 2:1, 0 °C to RT) afforded the carboxylic acid 15 in 94%

thought.background: Cycloadduct 11 is available. Need to reduce the imide to alcohol 16.
thought.decision: To reduce 11, chose lithium borohydride, expecting good yield.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, reduction of 11 with lithium borohydride (LiBH4) provided the alcohol 16 in 85% yield.

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 15 is available. The neighboring carboxylic acid group could direct hydroboration at C7'.
thought.gap: Need to perform hydroboration, selective protection, and oxidation to form ketone 17.
thought.decision: To form ketone 17, chose BH3·THF followed by NaBO3·4H2O for hydroboration, then selective TBS protection and DMP oxidation, expecting good overall yield and regioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Ketone 17 and its regioisomer 18 were obtained in 89% overall yield (rr = 3.43) via a sequence of selective o-protection and oxidation. The absolute configuration of 17 was determined by X-ray.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of 15 with BH3·THF (-30 °C to RT.) followed by the addition of NaBO3·4H2O furnished the mixed diol product resulting from the concomitant reduction of the carboxylic acid. Without purification, this diol was converted to ketone 17 and its regioisomer 18 in 89% overall yield (rr = 3.43) via a sequence of selective o-protection and oxidation. The absolute configuration of the tricycle ketone 17 was determined by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

thought.background: Alcohol 16 is available. The neighboring hydroxy group could potentially direct hydroboration at C7'.
thought.gap: Need to perform hydroboration, selective protection, and oxidation to form ketone 17.
thought.decision: To form ketone 17, chose BH3·THF followed by NaBO3·4H2O for hydroboration, then selective protection and oxidation, expecting good overall yield and regioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Applying the same conditions to alcohol 16, a mixture of two regioisomeric ketones was isolated in 1:1 ratio, indicating that the carboxylic acid is a better directing group.

evidence.text_span: Notably, applying the same conditions to alcohol 16, a mixture of two regioisomeric ketones was isolated in 1:1 ratio. These results indicated that carboxylic acid is a better-directing group in the hydroboration step.

thought.background: Ketone 17 is available. Need to convert it to vinyl triflate 10 for Suzuki coupling.
thought.gap: Standard enol triflation conditions should work.
thought.decision: To form vinyl triflate 10, chose deprotonation with KHMDS followed by addition of PhNTf2, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: Vinyl triflate 10 was obtained in 94% isolated yield.

evidence.text_span: Deprotonation of ketone 17 with potassium bis(trimethylsilyl) amide (KHMDS) at -78 °C followed by the addition of PhNTf2 afforded the desired vinyl triflate 10 in 94% isolated yield.

thought.background: Vinyl triflate 10 is available. Need to couple it with 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester 19 to form precursor 20 for model studies.
thought.gap: Standard Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling conditions should work.
thought.decision: To form 20, chose Pd-catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling with Pd(dppf)Cl2 and K3CO3, expecting good yield.

evidence.text_span: The precursor 20, generated through Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling between 10 and commercially available 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester 19, was screened in various acidic conditions for the key transformation.

thought.background: Precursor 20 is available. Need to test the key O-deprotection/Prins cyclization/Cycloetherification sequence.
thought.gap: Identify suitable acidic conditions for the transformation.
thought.decision: To test the key transformation, chose HCl in DCM at -10 °C, expecting the desired product 21.

outcome.observation: The desirable 21 was obtained in the presence of HCl (10 equiv) in DCM at -10 °C in 89% yield with 20:1 diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Gratifyingly, the desirable 21 was obtained in the presence of HCl (10 equiv) in DCM at -10 °C in 89% yield with 20:1 diastereoselectivity (X-ray of major rac-21).

thought.background: Vinyl triflate 10 is available. Need to couple it with boronic acid 9 to form tetracycle 8.
thought.gap: Standard Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling conditions should work.
thought.decision: To form 8, chose Pd-catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling with Pd(dppf)Cl2, S-Phos, and K3PO4, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Functionalized tetracycle 8 was obtained in 69% yield.

evidence.text_span: The Pd-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling between 10 and the known boronic acid 9 proceeded smoothly to afford the functionalized tetracycle 8 in 69% yield.

thought.background: Tetracycle 8 is available. The model reaction (step 9) showed that HCl in DCM at -10 °C worked well for precursor 20.
thought.gap: Need to apply the O-deprotection/Prins cyclization/Cycloetherification sequence to 8.
thought.decision: To convert 8 to 22, chose HCl (10 equiv) in DCM at -10 °C, expecting the desired product.

outcome.observation: Tetracycle 8 is unstable and decomposed under the identical conditions that worked for the model reaction.

evidence.text_span: However, tetracycle 8 is unstable and was decomposed when treated with the aforementioned identical condition.

thought.background: The previous attempt to convert 8 to 22 using the model reaction conditions failed due to decomposition. The transformation is challenging, and different acidic conditions need to be screened.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions (acid, temperature, time) to achieve the O-deprotection/Prins cyclization/Cycloetherification sequence for 8 without decomposition or significant by-product formation.
thought.decision: To achieve the transformation, chose to screen various Brønsted and Lewis acids in different solvents and temperatures. The optimal conditions were identified as HCl (10 equiv) in DCM at -78 °C for 24h, expecting to minimize by-product formation and achieve good yield of 22.

outcome.observation: Under optimized conditions (HCl (2 M in ethyl acetate, 10 equiv) in DCM at -78 °C for 24 h), 22 was generated as a pair of diastereoisomers in 86% yield. The reaction temperature and time had to be carefully controlled to avoid by-product formation.

evidence.text_span: The desired product as a pair of diastereoisomers 22 could be isolated under a series of acidic conditions with the concomitant formation of Prins cyclization product 23. To minimize the generation of by-products, different Brønsted acids [TFA, HCl, p-TsOH·H2O, (–)-CSA] and Lewis acids [BF3·Et2O, AlCl3, Sc(OTf)3, TBSOTf, TMSOTf, etc.] in different solvents at various temperatures were screened (Fig. 5). HCl was still identified as the most suitable promotor. The optimum reaction conditions consisted of stirring a DCM solution of 8 in the presence of an excess of HCl (2 M in ethyl acetate, 10 equiv) at -78 °C for 24 h (Fig. 5, Entry 11). Under these conditions, 22 was generated as a pair of diastereoisomers in 86% yield, which could also be directly oxidized by Dess–Martin periodinane to afford the desired product 7 in a one-pot sequence in 80% overall yield (Fig. 6).

thought.background: Hexacyclic intermediate 22 is available. It can be directly oxidized to 7 in a one-pot sequence.
thought.decision: To oxidize 22 to 7, chose Dess–Martin periodinane and NaHCO3 in DCM, expecting good overall yield.

outcome.observation: The desired product 7 was obtained in 80% overall yield. The structure and absolute configuration of 7 was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

evidence.text_span: Under these conditions, 22 was generated as a pair of diastereoisomers in 86% yield, which could also be directly oxidized by Dess–Martin periodinane to afford the desired product 7 in a one-pot sequence in 80% overall yield (Fig. 6). The structure and absolute configuration of 7 was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

thought.background: Hexacycle 7 is available. Need to perform Fleming–Tamao oxidation to obtain diol 26.
thought.gap: Standard Fleming–Tamao oxidation conditions should work.
thought.decision: To obtain diol 26, chose KF, H2O2, and KHCO3 in methanol/THF, expecting good yield.

evidence.text_span: The Fleming–Tamao oxidation of 7 (KF, H2O2, methanol/THF, v/v = 1:1) afforded diol 26 in 91% yield54.

thought.background: Diol 26 is available. Need to dehydrate it to form product 27.
thought.decision: To dehydrate 26, chose Grieco's procedure using 2-nitrophenylselenocyanate, Py, PBu3, and H2O2 in THF, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Desired product 27 was obtained in 50% isolated yield. The structure was confirmed by X-ray.

evidence.text_span: Dehydration of 26 underwent smoothly to give the desired product 27 (X-ray) in 50% isolated yield according to Grieco’s procedure55.

thought.background: Product 27 contains a vinyl group that needs to be converted to a methyl ketone. This is a challenging late-stage Wacker oxidation.
thought.gap: Standard Wacker oxidation conditions might lead to undesired products due to steric hindrance.
thought.decision: To convert the vinyl group to a methyl ketone, chose classic Wacker oxidation conditions, expecting the desired product.

outcome.observation: Under classic conditions, aldehyde resulting from the anti-Markovnikov addition was formed as a major product, presumably due to steric effect.

evidence.text_span: As Kawamoto and Ito mentioned29, the late-stage Wacker oxidation of the vinyl group 27 to methyl ketone group 28 proved to be challenging. Under classic conditions, aldehyde resulting from the anti-Markovnikov addition was formed as a major product due presumably to the steric effect56.

thought.background: Classic Wacker oxidation failed to convert the hindered vinyl group of 27 to methyl ketone 28, yielding an undesired aldehyde. Iron-catalyzed Wacker-type oxidation has been reported for similar transformations.
thought.gap: Need to find suitable conditions for the Wacker-type oxidation of the hindered vinyl group.
thought.decision: To convert the vinyl group to methyl ketone 28, chose iron-catalyzed Wacker-type oxidation conditions developed by Han's and Knölker's groups, expecting the desired methyl ketone.

outcome.observation: Methylketone 28 was obtained in 65% yield using iron-catalyzed Wacker-type oxidation.

evidence.text_span: We then turned our attention to the iron-catalyzed Wacker-type oxidation of olefins to ketones developed by Han’s57 and Knölker’s58 groups, respectively. Gratefully, applying their standard conditions to 27 [Fe(dbm)3, PhSiH3, EtOH, RT], methylketone 28 was obtained in 65% yield.

thought.background: Methylketone 28 is available. The final step is O-demethylation to obtain (-)-lucidumone.
thought.decision: To obtain (-)-lucidumone, chose Lewis acid catalyzed double O-demethylation using AlCl3 and 1-dodecanethiol in DCM, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: (-)-lucidumone was obtained in 70% isolated yield. The physical and spectroscopic data are identical to the natural product.

evidence.text_span: Finally, Lewis acid catalyzed double O-demethylation of 28 afforded (–)-lucidumone (–)-1 in 70% isolated yield. The physical and spectroscopic data of the synthetic (–)-lucidumone (–)-1 are identical to those reported for the natural product.

validation.significance: This work reports an enantioselective total synthesis of (-)-lucidumone, a complex Ganoderma meroterpenoid with potential anti-inflammatory activity. The synthesis features several key transformations, including a Cu-catalyzed enantioselective silicon-tethered intramolecular Diels-Alder cycloaddition, a Brønsted acid promoted tandem O-deprotection/Prins cyclization/Cycloetherification/oxidation sequence, Fleming-Tamao oxidation, and an iron-catalyzed Wacker-type oxidation of a hindered vinyl group. The successful synthesis provides access to this biologically active natural product and demonstrates novel synthetic methodologies for complex polycyclic structures.

source.title: Enantioselective total synthesis of (‒)-lucidumone enabled by tandem prins cyclization/cycloetherification sequence.
source.authors: Liao XZ, Wang R, Wang X, Li G.
